front: Poem: the speaker sounds a 'barbaric yawp over the roofs of the world' and says 'I am large, I contain multitudes'
Poem: a child asks 'What is the grass?' and is told it is 'the beautiful uncut hair of graves' and 'a uniform hieroglyphic'
Poem: section 11 describes twenty-eight young men bathing by the shore, watched by a woman behind blinds who imagines joining them
Poem: grass is called 'the handkerchief of the Lord,' 'the flag of my disposition,' and 'the produced babe of vegetation'
Poem: a hermit thrush sings a 'Song of the bleeding throat' while a 'great star early droop'd in the western sky' allegorizes a fallen president
back: When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd (Walt Whitman)
work: When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd

front: Poem: the ship is 'anchor'd safe and sound, its voyage closed and done' but its captain has 'fallen cold and dead'
Poem: opens 'Flood-tide below me! I watch you face to face' and describes commuters 'attired in the usual costumes'
Poem: a mockingbird calls for its lost mate as the speaker hears 'the low and delicious word death' and 'the sea whisper'd me'
back: Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking (Walt Whitman)
work: Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking

front: Poem: the speaker imagines 'poking among the meats in the refrigerator and eyeing the grocery boys' while asking 'Which way does your beard point tonight?'
Work: homoerotic 'Calamus' poems celebrate 'the manly love of comrades'; originally published as the sequence 'Live Oak with Moss'
Poem: the soul is compared to a spider on a 'little promontory,' launching 'filament, filament, filament, out of itself'
Author: used the phrenological terms 'amativeness' and 'adhesiveness' to distinguish between types of attraction; coded companion Peter Doyle as '16.4' in notebooks
Author: his 'cult' among Latin American modernists began with an 1887 Jose Marti essay; Ruben Dario coined the nickname 'el gran viejo'
Author: Vaughan Williams's A Sea Symphony opens with a B-flat minor brass fanfare on the word 'Behold' and sets texts by this poet
Poem: describes the 'varied carols' of 'mechanics...blithe and strong,' carpenters, boatmen, and shoemakers singing in America
Poem: a dream-vision features a 'beautiful gigantic swimmer' killed by the sea, Washington weeping at Brooklyn, and a 'red squaw' visiting the mother
Author: wrote the early temperance novel Franklin Evans about a country boy named Colby who descends into alcoholism; later called it 'damned rot'
